summary:
Voltaire (François-Marie Arouet, 1694–1778 AD) was the most celebrated and influential of the French philosophes — the writers, thinkers, and public intellectuals who made the [[event:2346|Enlightenment]] a popular cultural movement rather than merely an academic exercise, using wit, satire, fiction, and the periodical press to spread ideas of religious tolerance, rational governance, and individual liberty to educated audiences across Europe. Voltaire's satirical novel Candide (1759) — written as a devastating response to the [[event:2387|Lisbon earthquake's]] challenge to optimistic theodicy — demonstrated that philosophical ideas could be conveyed through accessible narrative, while his historical works (The Age of Louis XIV, Essay on the Manners and Spirit of Nations) pioneered a broader, more secular conception of history that included culture, economy, and religion rather than focusing exclusively on kings and battles.

Voltaire's crusading campaigns for individual victims of religious intolerance — particularly his defense of Jean Calas (a Protestant merchant falsely convicted of murdering his son to prevent conversion to Catholicism, broken on the wheel in 1762) — demonstrated how a writer could mobilize public opinion to challenge judicial injustice, effectively inventing the concept of the public intellectual campaigning for individual rights. His letters to [[event:2349|Catherine the Great]], [[event:2386|Frederick the Great]], and other European monarchs exemplified the philosophes' strategy of cultivating 'enlightened despots' who might implement reform from above. His deism ('God created the universe and wound it up like a clock, then left it alone'), his fierce anti-clericalism ('Écrasez l'infâme!' — Crush the infamous thing! — his motto against religious intolerance and institutional religion), and his belief in civil tolerance became defining positions of progressive thought throughout Europe and America.
